multiple_choice.text: A **joint-stock** company is a special type of business. What makes it different from other companies?
options: Only the government can own it.
Ownership is split into shares that people can buy and sell.
The owners must pay all company debts with their own money.
It cannot sell shares to the public.
multiple_choice.answer: Ownership is split into shares that people can buy and sell.

multiple_choice.text: At a company's annual meeting, what do **shareholders** usually do?
options: They manage the company every day.
They check the accounts of other companies.
They vote on important decisions and choose the board of directors.
They write contracts for new workers.
multiple_choice.answer: They vote on important decisions and choose the board of directors.

multiple_choice.text: Why do people often criticise large **multinationals** about taxes?
options: They only sell products in one country.
They are often accused of moving profits to countries with low taxes.
They do not have enough money to pay lawyers.
They are not allowed to trade in other countries.
multiple_choice.answer: They are often accused of moving profits to countries with low taxes.

multiple_choice.text: A big company creates several **subsidiaries**. What does this mean?
options: These companies are fully independent with no parent company.
These companies are controlled or owned by a parent company.
The company is closing and selling everything.
All the companies have joined into one.
multiple_choice.answer: These companies are controlled or owned by a parent company.

multiple_choice.text: Which of these is an example of an intangible **asset**?
options: Office chairs and computers.
Cash and gold.
Patents, trademarks, and brand reputation.
Unpaid bills and bank loans.
multiple_choice.answer: Patents, trademarks, and brand reputation.

multiple_choice.text: Two competing companies decide to make a horizontal **merger**. What usually happens?
options: They get a bigger market share and can work better together.
The price of all products goes down straight away.
Both companies close their boards of directors.
They must become a non-profit organisation.
multiple_choice.answer: They get a bigger market share and can work better together.

multiple_choice.text: What makes a hostile **acquisition** different from a friendly one?
options: No money is exchanged between the companies.
The management of the target company does not want the takeover.
It only happens between small family businesses.
The buying company helps the other company pay its debts.
multiple_choice.answer: The management of the target company does not want the takeover.

multiple_choice.text: **Dotcom** companies became very popular in the late 1990s. What kind of businesses were they?
options: Heavy industry and steel factories.
Farm product exporters.
Internet and online shopping businesses.
Traditional banks with physical branches.
multiple_choice.answer: Internet and online shopping businesses.

multiple_choice.text: Which sentence best describes a successful **entrepreneur**?
options: They try to avoid all risks in business.
They find gaps in the market and create new ideas, even if they might fail.
They only follow rules set by big companies.
They only focus on short-term work and have no long-term plan.
multiple_choice.answer: They find gaps in the market and create new ideas, even if they might fail.

multiple_choice.text: A good leader tries to **inspire** their team. What does 'inspire' mean here?
options: To force people to work harder by threatening them.
To give people the confidence and desire to do something great.
To pay workers more than other companies.
To ignore the mistakes that employees make.
multiple_choice.answer: To give people the confidence and desire to do something great.

multiple_choice.text: Companies give bonuses and awards to **motivate** staff. What is the main goal?
options: To reduce the number of workers.
To make employees feel bored with their work.
To give employees a reason to work well.
To stop employees from talking to each other.
multiple_choice.answer: To give employees a reason to work well.

multiple_choice.text: HR departments organise workshops to **develop talent** in the company. Why is this important?
options: To keep employees at the same skill level for many years.
To find and improve the skills and abilities of employees.
To reduce the company's monthly budget.
To replace all workers with machines.
multiple_choice.answer: To find and improve the skills and abilities of employees.

multiple_choice.text: Which of these best describes an autocratic **management style**?
options: Employees can make all the decisions on their own.
Everyone in the team has an equal vote.
One person makes all the decisions without asking the team.
There are no clear rules or structure in the workplace.
multiple_choice.answer: One person makes all the decisions without asking the team.

multiple_choice.text: A manager decides to **delegate** some tasks. What does this mean?
options: The manager does all the hard work alone.
The manager gives responsibilities to other team members.
The manager cancels all difficult projects.
The manager asks customers to fill in company forms.
multiple_choice.answer: The manager gives responsibilities to other team members.

multiple_choice.text: A manager looks after the work of their **subordinates**. Who are these people?
options: People who own most of the company's shares.
Consultants who work for a different firm.
Employees who have a lower position than the manager.
The board of directors who oversee the CEO.
multiple_choice.answer: Employees who have a lower position than the manager.

multiple_choice.text: A manager is given the **authority** to sign contracts. What does this mean?
options: They have the legal right and power to make decisions.
They are physically strong enough to move office equipment.
They have a lot of free time to read documents.
They want to become a professional lawyer.
multiple_choice.answer: They have the legal right and power to make decisions.

multiple_choice.text: A company makes a long-term plan to sell only eco-friendly luxury products. What is this type of plan called?
options: Budget
Strategy
Transaction
Department
multiple_choice.answer: Strategy

multiple_choice.text: What is the **planning process** in a business?
options: Hiring new staff for the reception.
A set of steps to set goals and find the best way to reach them.
Building a new office.
Reading emails and answering calls every day.
multiple_choice.answer: A set of steps to set goals and find the best way to reach them.

multiple_choice.text: A tech company talks about 'emerging **markets**'. What do they usually mean?
options: Grocery shops in the city centre.
New groups of customers or regions where the economy is growing.
Documents needed to export goods.
Internal software used to track staff.
multiple_choice.answer: New groups of customers or regions where the economy is growing.

multiple_choice.text: What usually happens when there is strong **competition** in a healthy economy?
options: Prices go up and quality goes down.
Only one company survives in the market.
Products improve and services become more creative.
All advertising and marketing stops.
multiple_choice.answer: Products improve and services become more creative.

multiple_choice.text: In a SWOT analysis, a new law that helps your company's technology is an example of which category?
options: Weaknesses
Threats
Opportunities
Liabilities
multiple_choice.answer: Opportunities

multiple_choice.text: A startup shows 20% **growth** in revenue this year. What does this tell us?
options: The company is getting smaller.
The company is making more money and expanding.
The company is closing its international offices.
The company is selling fewer products.
multiple_choice.answer: The company is making more money and expanding.

multiple_choice.text: New competitors entering the market and an economic recession are examples of external ________ for a business.
options: Threats
Assets
Promotions
Bonuses
multiple_choice.answer: Threats

multiple_choice.text: What is the key difference between daily tasks and **strategic objectives**?
options: Strategic objectives are small jobs that take only a few minutes.
Strategic objectives are long-term goals that match the company's vision.
Daily tasks are only done by the CEO.
There is no difference between them.
multiple_choice.answer: Strategic objectives are long-term goals that match the company's vision.

multiple_choice.text: A manager needs to allocate **resources** for a new project. What can 'resources' include?
options: Only the bins in the office.
Money, time, staff, and equipment.
The personal hobbies of employees' families.
The weather outside the factory.
multiple_choice.answer: Money, time, staff, and equipment.

multiple_choice.text: In business language, who does the term **'fat cats'** usually refer to?
options: Successful new interns
Executives who are paid too much
Loyal long-term customers
Hardworking factory staff
multiple_choice.answer: Executives who are paid too much

multiple_choice.text: A CEO's **pay package** normally includes more than just a salary. What else is usually included?
options: Free office supplies
Stock options and bonuses
Unpaid extra work hours
Personal grocery bills
multiple_choice.answer: Stock options and bonuses

multiple_choice.text: Which of these is a non-monetary **reward** for an employee?
options: A cash prize
A monthly salary increase
Extra days off work
A formal debt notice
multiple_choice.answer: Extra days off work

multiple_choice.text: What is the main purpose of a **pension** plan?
options: To pay for daily transport to work
To give you money to live on after you retire
To fund a short holiday abroad
To buy new furniture for the office
multiple_choice.answer: To give you money to live on after you retire

multiple_choice.text: In business, what is a **'golden parachute'**?
options: A private jet for business travel
A large payment an executive receives if they lose their job
Free skydiving lessons for managers
A very expensive office chair
multiple_choice.answer: A large payment an executive receives if they lose their job

multiple_choice.text: When an employee leaves a company, they may agree on a **severance deal**. What is it?
options: A new contract to stay at the company longer
An agreement about the money the employee receives when they leave
A list of their responsibilities in a future job
A financial penalty for quitting the job
multiple_choice.answer: An agreement about the money the employee receives when they leave

multiple_choice.text: What is the main job of **remuneration committees** in a company?
options: To set the price of company products
To decide how much senior managers should be paid
To plan the office cleaning schedule
To choose which software the IT team should buy
multiple_choice.answer: To decide how much senior managers should be paid

multiple_choice.text: What do **compensation committees** do inside a large company?
options: They manage daily tax reports and accounting.
They set salaries, bonuses, and benefits for top executives.
They plan social events and office parties.
They give legal help to employees in personal cases.
multiple_choice.answer: They set salaries, bonuses, and benefits for top executives.

multiple_choice.text: Regular exercise has many ________ for your health, such as less stress and better sleep.
options: habits
benefits
obstacles
symptoms
multiple_choice.answer: benefits

multiple_choice.text: Japan is called an MEDC. What does this tell us about the country?
options: It has a very high birth rate.
It mostly depends on farming.
It has a high standard of living and good infrastructure.
It receives a lot of money from other countries as aid.
multiple_choice.answer: It has a high standard of living and good infrastructure.

multiple_choice.text: Which of these is a common problem in an LEDC?
options: High income per person
Good public healthcare for everyone
Not enough clean water for people
Low unemployment rates
multiple_choice.answer: Not enough clean water for people

multiple_choice.text: Oil, clean water, and timber (wood) are all examples of ________.
options: manufactured goods
natural resources
industrial waste
economic policies
multiple_choice.answer: natural resources

multiple_choice.text: Big construction projects like dams and motorways often harm the environment. What is the most common problem?
options: They reduce carbon emissions.
They improve soil quality.
They destroy natural habitats.
They increase the number of plants and animals.
multiple_choice.answer: They destroy natural habitats.

multiple_choice.text: Factories, power plants, and oil refineries are all examples of ________.
options: residential zones
farming units
industrial facilities
commercial markets
multiple_choice.answer: industrial facilities

multiple_choice.text: What is a country's Gross Domestic Product (GDP)?
options: The total foreign aid the country receives each year.
The average salary earned by citizens.
The total value of all goods and services produced in a country.
The total number of people who have jobs.
multiple_choice.answer: The total value of all goods and services produced in a country.

multiple_choice.text: Groups of ________ are protesting against the new oil pipeline because it could damage local ecosystems.
options: economists
urbanists
geographers
environmentalists
multiple_choice.answer: environmentalists

multiple_choice.text: One major result of fast industrialisation in LEDCs is more air and water ________.
options: tourism
agriculture
education
pollution
multiple_choice.answer: pollution

multiple_choice.text: Illegal cutting of forests **endangers** many rare plants and animals. What does 'endangers' mean?
options: protects
threatens
supports
increases
multiple_choice.answer: threatens

multiple_choice.text: A company uses the **marketing mix** to plan how to sell its product. What is the marketing mix?
options: A list of company employees and their roles.
A plan that uses key elements together to promote and sell a product.
A financial report that shows monthly profits.
A legal paper that protects a brand's name.
multiple_choice.answer: A plan that uses key elements together to promote and sell a product.

multiple_choice.text: The **Four Ps** is a well-known marketing framework. Which group correctly lists all Four Ps?
options: Product, Price, Promotion, Place
People, Profit, Plan, Promotion
Price, Publicity, Production, Place
Product, Profit, People, Platform
multiple_choice.answer: Product, Price, Promotion, Place

multiple_choice.text: When a company designs a new ________, it must think about quality, packaging, and what the customer needs.
options: invoice
product
regulation
contract
multiple_choice.answer: product

multiple_choice.text: A luxury brand sets a very high ________ for its handbags to show exclusivity and quality.
options: discount
quantity
price
deadline
multiple_choice.answer: price

multiple_choice.text: A company ran a **promotion** campaign using social media and famous people. What is the main goal of promotion?
options: To lower the cost of making a product.
To make more people aware of the product and want to buy it.
To improve the quality of the product.
To find new suppliers for materials.
multiple_choice.answer: To make more people aware of the product and want to buy it.

multiple_choice.text: In the marketing mix, what does '**place**' refer to?
options: Where the company's head office is located.
The country where the product is manufactured.
How and where customers can find and buy the product.
The physical size of the product.
multiple_choice.answer: How and where customers can find and buy the product.

multiple_choice.text: Customers chose this phone because of its great ________, like long battery life and a high-quality camera.
options: regulations
competitors
features
liabilities
multiple_choice.answer: features

multiple_choice.text: The company uses warehouses and delivery trucks for the **distribution** of its goods. What does 'distribution' mean here?
options: The design and packaging of a product.
The process of getting products from the maker to the customer.
The advertising method used to promote a product.
The way a company decides the price of a product.
multiple_choice.answer: The process of getting products from the maker to the customer.

multiple_choice.text: Apple's design and its loyal customers give it a strong ________ over other companies.
options: competitive advantage
financial deficit
trade restriction
overhead cost
multiple_choice.answer: competitive advantage

multiple_choice.text: Famous ________ like Nike and Coca-Cola spend millions every year to keep their image strong.
options: regulations
sectors
brands
subsidies
multiple_choice.answer: brands

multiple_choice.text: Luxury car adverts often show a lifestyle of success to connect with customers' ________.
options: complaints
regulations
debts
aspirations
multiple_choice.answer: aspirations

multiple_choice.text: ________ has made it easier for companies to sell products and do business in other countries.
options: Privatisation
Globalisation
Regulation
Inflation
multiple_choice.answer: Globalisation

multiple_choice.text: One key reason companies move production abroad is to achieve **cost savings**. What does this mean?
options: Spending more money on quality control.
Spending less money on production.
Hiring more skilled workers.
Entering new international markets.
multiple_choice.answer: Spending less money on production.

multiple_choice.text: Rent, electricity bills, and staff wages are all examples of a company's ________.
options: revenues
assets
operating costs
investments
multiple_choice.answer: operating costs

multiple_choice.text: Instead of hiring its own IT staff, the firm uses **outsourcing** for IT support. What is outsourcing?
options: Training your own employees in new skills.
Paying an external company to do a job for you.
Moving the head office to another country.
Selling part of the business to investors.
multiple_choice.answer: Paying an external company to do a job for you.

multiple_choice.text: A ________ can be a small local shop or a large corporation — any organisation that sells goods or services.
options: charity
business
government
union
multiple_choice.answer: business

multiple_choice.text: The company automated its internal ________, such as invoicing and stock management, to save time.
options: processes
brands
liabilities
assets
multiple_choice.answer: processes

multiple_choice.text: Customers can phone our **call centre** 24 hours a day to get help. What is a call centre?
options: A factory that produces phone equipment.
An office where staff answer a large number of phone calls.
A shop that sells mobile phones.
A place where sales staff receive their training.
multiple_choice.answer: An office where staff answer a large number of phone calls.

multiple_choice.text: The business hired external ________ to take care of its cleaning, security, and food services.
options: auditors
shareholders
service providers
regulators
multiple_choice.answer: service providers

multiple_choice.text: Moving a company's operations to another country to reduce costs is called ________.
options: merging
offshoring
licensing
franchising
multiple_choice.answer: offshoring

multiple_choice.text: Entering data, processing payroll, and keeping records are typical ________ jobs that companies often outsource.
options: front-office
back-office
executive
marketing
multiple_choice.answer: back-office

multiple_choice.text: The sports centre offers excellent ________, including a gym, a swimming pool, and conference rooms.
options: liabilities
policies
facilities
dividends
multiple_choice.answer: facilities

multiple_choice.text: Many companies move their factories to Southeast Asia because ________ there are much lower than in Europe.
options: trade tariffs
tax rates
labour costs
interest rates
multiple_choice.answer: labour costs

multiple_choice.text: The company closed mainly because of **mismanagement**. What does this suggest?
options: The company was run very efficiently.
The people in charge made poor decisions.
The company had too many employees.
The company expanded too quickly into new markets.
multiple_choice.answer: The people in charge made poor decisions.

multiple_choice.text: All public companies must publish accurate **financial reporting** every year. What is the main reason?
options: To advertise their products to new customers.
To attract talented new employees.
To keep investors and regulators properly informed.
To apply for grants from the government.
multiple_choice.answer: To keep investors and regulators properly informed.

multiple_choice.text: The company brought in independent ________ to check if its financial accounts were accurate.
options: auditors
marketers
recruiters
consultants
multiple_choice.answer: auditors

multiple_choice.text: The SEC is a US government body. What is its main role?
options: Setting interest rates for banks.
Controlling financial markets and protecting investors.
Collecting income tax from businesses.
Approving new product launches by companies.
multiple_choice.answer: Controlling financial markets and protecting investors.

multiple_choice.text: A company's **balance sheet** shows its financial situation at a specific point in time. What information does it include?
options: A record of daily sales numbers.
What the company owns and what it owes.
A list of all employees and their salaries.
Reports about customer satisfaction.
multiple_choice.answer: What the company owns and what it owes.

multiple_choice.text: **Income statements** show a company's revenue and costs over a period of time. What do they measure?
options: The company's total debt.
How well the company performed financially.
The market value of the company's shares.
The number of products that were sold.
multiple_choice.answer: How well the company performed financially.

multiple_choice.text: The company's **profit accounts** showed strong results for the third year in a row. What do profit accounts record?
options: Money the company owes to its suppliers.
Money the company made after paying all its costs.
The total amount the company has borrowed.
Tax payments made to the government.
multiple_choice.answer: Money the company made after paying all its costs.

multiple_choice.text: The business closed because its **loss accounts** showed it had spent much more than it earned for two years. What are loss accounts?
options: Documents showing money the company gained.
Records showing money the company lost or negative results.
Reports about how productive employees were.
Summaries of the company's marketing costs.
multiple_choice.answer: Records showing money the company lost or negative results.

multiple_choice.text: The small company had problems because its monthly ________ — rent, salaries, and bills — were more than its income.
options: revenues
outgoings
assets
profits
multiple_choice.answer: outgoings

multiple_choice.text: The **human resources** department is responsible for which area?
options: Managing the company's finances and accounts.
Finding, training, and managing staff.
Developing new products for the market.
Handling complaints from customers.
multiple_choice.answer: Finding, training, and managing staff.

multiple_choice.text: All ________ must complete a safety training course in their first week at the company.
options: clients
shareholders
personnel
suppliers
multiple_choice.answer: personnel

multiple_choice.text: The company got over 200 applications from **candidates** after posting the job advert. Who are candidates?
options: People who train new employees.
People who are applying for a job.
People who inspect workplaces for safety.
People who manage the company's finances.
multiple_choice.answer: People who are applying for a job.

multiple_choice.text: Please send your **curriculum vitae** with your application. What is a curriculum vitae?
options: A letter explaining why you want the job.
A document that shows your education and work history.
A list of people who can recommend you.
A certificate that proves your qualifications.
multiple_choice.answer: A document that shows your education and work history.

multiple_choice.text: In the US, job seekers often send a **résumé** instead of a full CV. What is the main difference?
options: It includes a photo of the applicant.
It is longer and more detailed than a CV.
It is a short summary, usually just one page.
It is written by the employer, not the applicant.
multiple_choice.answer: It is a short summary, usually just one page.

multiple_choice.text: The job requires specific **qualifications**, such as an engineering degree or a professional certificate. What are qualifications?
options: Formal achievements that prove your knowledge or skill.
Personal hobbies listed on a CV.
The total years someone has worked.
Recommendations from past employers.
multiple_choice.answer: Formal achievements that prove your knowledge or skill.

multiple_choice.text: She got the job because of her five years of ________ in project management.
options: ambition
experience
connections
training
multiple_choice.answer: experience

multiple_choice.text: Employers look for candidates with strong communication **skills**. What are skills?
options: Formal certificates from universities.
Natural or learned abilities to do something well.
The number of years spent working at one company.
Personal interests that someone has outside of work.
multiple_choice.answer: Natural or learned abilities to do something well.

multiple_choice.text: The team **screens** all applications before inviting anyone to interview. What does 'screens' mean here?
options: Rejects all applicants immediately.
Reviews and filters applications to find the most suitable ones.
Sends job offers to all candidates at once.
Posts all applications online for the public to see.
multiple_choice.answer: Reviews and filters applications to find the most suitable ones.

multiple_choice.text: The company received over 300 **applications** for the marketing position within 48 hours. What are applications?
options: Complaints sent by unhappy customers.
Forms or documents sent by people who want the job.
Invoices sent by suppliers.
Contracts agreed with business clients.
multiple_choice.answer: Forms or documents sent by people who want the job.

multiple_choice.text: After three rounds of interviews, Sarah was **hired** as the new operations manager. What does 'hired' mean?
options: She was dismissed from her position.
She was offered and accepted the job.
She was promoted within the company.
She was moved to a different department.
multiple_choice.answer: She was offered and accepted the job.

multiple_choice.text: The finance team manages the company's **payroll** every month. What does payroll involve?
options: Tracking payments received from customers.
Calculating and paying employee salaries.
Managing the company's long-term investments.
Preparing the company's annual budget.
multiple_choice.answer: Calculating and paying employee salaries.

multiple_choice.text: Police arrested a group of **counterfeiters** who had been making fake designer goods for years. Who are counterfeiters?
options: People who legally manufacture goods in factories.
People who illegally copy and sell fake products.
People who check the quality of products.
People who create original product designs.
multiple_choice.answer: People who illegally copy and sell fake products.

multiple_choice.text: The inventor registered several ________ to stop competitors from copying her new technology.
options: trademarks
licences
patents
copyrights
multiple_choice.answer: patents

multiple_choice.text: Companies spend a lot of money to protect their **intellectual property**. What does this mean?
options: Keeping physical goods safe inside warehouses.
Legally protecting original ideas, inventions, and creative work.
Training employees to use new technology.
Preventing skilled workers from leaving the company.
multiple_choice.answer: Legally protecting original ideas, inventions, and creative work.

multiple_choice.text: The author's book is protected by **copyright**, so others cannot copy it without permission. What is copyright?
options: A legal right that protects original creative work.
A certificate that proves academic qualifications.
A contract signed between two businesses.
A government tax on the creative industries.
multiple_choice.answer: A legal right that protects original creative work.

multiple_choice.text: The golden arches logo and the name 'McDonald's' are both registered **trademarks**. What do trademarks protect?
options: An inventor's technical designs.
A brand's unique symbols, names, or logos.
An author's written content.
A company's private financial records.
multiple_choice.answer: A brand's unique symbols, names, or logos.

multiple_choice.text: Some markets are full of **fakes** — items made to look like expensive brands but sold at a low price. What are fakes?
options: Original limited-edition products.
Counterfeit or imitation products.
Genuine products sold at a discount.
Second-hand branded items.
multiple_choice.answer: Counterfeit or imitation products.

multiple_choice.text: The tech company took legal action against a rival for copyright **infringements**. What are infringements?
options: Formal business agreements between two companies.
Actions that break or violate someone's legal rights.
Financial penalties paid to the government.
Requests to register new patents.
multiple_choice.answer: Actions that break or violate someone's legal rights.

multiple_choice.text: Hospitals are using **digitisation** to store patient records more easily. What is digitisation?
options: Printing and storing paper documents in folders.
Converting information into a digital format.
Deleting old files permanently from computers.
Sharing printed files between different departments.
multiple_choice.answer: Converting information into a digital format.

multiple_choice.text: The company introduced secure **file sharing** systems so employees could work on documents from anywhere. What are file sharing systems?
options: Tools for sending and accessing files between users.
Physical folders for storing printed documents.
Programs that automatically delete old files.
Systems that monitor employee internet use.
multiple_choice.answer: Tools for sending and accessing files between users.

multiple_choice.text: **Illegal downloading** of films and music costs the entertainment industry billions of dollars each year. What is illegal downloading?
options: Watching content through official streaming apps.
Copying digital content without paying or getting permission.
Buying content legally from authorised websites.
Renting physical copies of films from a shop.
multiple_choice.answer: Copying digital content without paying or getting permission.

fill_blank.text: If many people buy shares in a business, it is called a j__________ company.

— Who decides if the company gets a new CEO?
— The s___________, because they own parts of the company.
M_____________ are companies that work in many different countries at the same time.
Apple, Google, and Amazon all own smaller companies in other countries. These smaller companies are called s___________.
Look at the balance sheet. The company's a_____ include its office building, computers, and $2 million in the bank.
BUSINESS NEWS: M______ between two rival supermarket chains create the country's largest food retailer.
The tech giant grew quickly, not by starting new products, but through a___________ — buying up smaller firms.
In the late 1990s, everyone was excited about d______. Then, in 2001, most of them crashed and lost all their money.
She left her job at the bank to start her own café. People like her, who take risks to build a business, are called e____________.
The best leaders don't just give orders — they i______ their team to want to do great work.

— Why did sales go up this month?
— We gave staff a bonus for every new client. It really helped m_______ them.
A manager's job is not only to complete tasks — it is also to d______ t_____ by helping employees grow and improve.
Sara's m_________ s____ is very relaxed — she trusts her team and never micromanages.
A manager who tries to do everything alone will fail. The best ones know how to d_______ work to the right people.
The director sent a message to all her s___________ asking them to attend the Monday meeting.
Only the project manager has the a________ to approve extra spending.
Before spending any money, the board agreed on a clear s_______ for the next three years.
Every autumn, the leadership team goes through a p_______ p______ to set goals for the coming year.
Our products sell well in Europe, but now we want to enter m______ in Southeast Asia.
There is strong c__________ in the smartphone industry — dozens of brands fight for customers every day.
The team spotted great o____________ in the new market — there were many customers and very few competitors.
